coredata.dc:title: Facile self-assembly of pyromellitic acid modified graphene oxide sheets into rhodamine B for highly selective luminescent sensing of Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiaoxiong
Zhang, Wenjun
Li, Cuiluo
Li, Yingjie
coredata.dc:description:
In this paper, a novel modified graphene oxide sheets (GOSs) has been synthesized based on pyromellitic acid (PMA) depositing on the surface of GOSs via noncovalent approach. Fourier Transform Infrared spectra (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) were adopted to investigate its structure. The as-prepared materials are dispersed in Rhodamine-B (RB) solutions which show highly efficient fluorescence. This phenomenon suggests the quenching effect of graphene oxide can be effectively weakened via rational structural modification. Moreover, this dispersion serving as a fluorescent probe still has a relatively high selectivity and sensitivity (Stern-Volmer constant K
SV = 4.58*104 L mol−1) to Fe3+ ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
Rhodamine B
Self-assembly
Efficiently fluorescence
Iron sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide-pyromellitic acid-Rhodamine B dispersion
Sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ with graphene oxide-pyromellitic acid-Rhodamine B dispersion

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Educational Committee of Hebei Province (No. ZD2017214 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of synthesis of GOSs-PMA.
FT-IR spectra of GO, PMA and GOSs-PMA.
The SEM images of GO (a) and GOSs-PMA (b).
The structural schematic diagram of GOSs.
XRD patterns of PMA, GO and GOSs-PMA.
Optical images of GO-RB (a), GOSs-PMA-RB (b) and RB (c), under 365 nm excitation.
Fluorescence emission spectra of GO-RB, GOSs-PMA-RB and RB, under 365 nm excitation.
Fluorescence emission spectra of GOSs-PMA-RB and GO-RB with different ratio of GO (1x: Original, 2x: Double), under 365 nm excitation.
Liquid FT-IR spectra of RB, GO-RB and GOSs-PMA-RB, which are dispersed in ethanol.
Optical images (top) and relative fluorescence intensity (bottom) of different metal ions with GOSs-PMA-RB dispersion, under 365 nm excitation.
Fluorescence emission spectra of RB, Na+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Pb2+ and Hg2+ with GOSs-PMA-RB dispersion, under 365 nm excitation.
Stern-Volmer plot of GOSs-PMA-RB quenched by Fe(NO3)3 aqueous solution.

The schematic diagram of GOSs-PMA-RB and Fe3+ ions reaction. The left blue circle is overlapping with GOSs-PMA chemical formula. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Facile self-assembly of pyromellitic acid modified graphene oxide sheets into rhodamine B for highly selective luminescent sensing of Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemical Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin, 300130, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this paper, a novel modified graphene oxide sheets (GOSs) has been synthesized based on pyromellitic acid (PMA) depositing on the surface of GOSs via noncovalent approach. Fourier Transform Infrared spectra (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) were adopted to investigate its structure. The as-prepared materials are dispersed in Rhodamine-B (RB) solutions which show highly efficient fluorescence. This phenomenon suggests the quenching effect of graphene oxide can be effectively weakened via rational structural modification. Moreover, this dispersion serving as a fluorescent probe still has a relatively high selectivity and sensitivity (Stern-Volmer constant K
SV = 4.58*104 L mol−1) to Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, graphene and graphene derivatives gradually attracted more attention due to their unique and excellent physical and chemical properties [1–3]. Graphene oxides, as a derivative of graphene, include many of the oxygen-containing functional groups. This structural characteristic make it serve as a platform interacting with various materials such as metal ions, organic small molecules, polymers and so on [4,5]. In luminescent materials, graphene and graphene derivatives mainly exhibit strong fluorescence quenching efficiency. Kim and his co-worker reported that graphene oxide will remain quenching the fluorescence of the dye molecule, even if there is polystyrene layer with 20 nm thickness in between graphene oxide and dye molecules [6]. Xie and his co-worker estimated that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of pure graphene can reach up to 1000 [7]. As for their quenching mechanism, some researchers has conducted researches in depth and assumed the following conclusions. The sp2 domains of graphene and graphene oxide can quench fluorescence of the light emitting group such as dyes, conjugated polymers and quantum dots via Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer [6]. And comparing graphene oxide, the quenching effect of reduced graphene oxide can be enhanced [8]. According to this quench characteristic of graphene and graphene oxide, researchers have applied them onto environmental and biological monitoring. Anju Mohan and his co-worker synthesized a graphene-rhodamine nanoprobe and this nanoprobe has a high sensitivity to Hg2+ [9].

Although the quenching effect of graphene and graphene oxide has an extremely unfavorable effect on some materials that need high luminescence intensity, some new graphene hybrid luminescent materials not only have not been quenched, but also enhance their properties [10–12]. This wonderful phenomenon draws the attention of many researchers. Inspired by these works, our team loaded Eu3+ complexes on GOSs by π-π stacking interaction and hydrogen bond interaction. This material shows a long decay lifetime of 1.82 ms, strong luminescence intensity and good thermal and photo stability [13]. From this work, we can conclude the right amount of GOSs can improve some performance of the luminescent material by reasonable self-assembly. As for interaction between PMA and GOSs, it is not clearly explained. In a recent work, we also obtained a new and strong luminescent material only via the interaction between PMA and GOSs. The interaction between PMA and GOSs has positive significance to weak the quenching effect of GOSs [14]. However, these works are limited to rare earth organic complexes, it is not known whether this positive effect can have the same effect in other materials, like dyes. Rhodamine dye is a common fluorescent dye, its fluorescence has the advantages of good light stability, high quantum yield, and its red fluorescence emission (500–700 nm) is less interfered by the background, becoming a kind of often selected fluorescent dye to test the quenching properties of new materials [15–18].

In this work, PMA was deposited on the surface of GOSs to fabricate modified GOSs, and then it was dispersed in Rhodamine B (RB) solution to test the quenching ability of this modified GOSs. We identified the quenching ability of this modified GOSs was obviously weakened under the same amount. As an exciting result, we think this phenomenon is important for the design of graphene oxide luminescent materials. At the same time, we also find out that the presence of this modified GOSs does not affect the luminescent groups of RB. This RB dispersion still has a relatively high selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ among 12 kinds of metal ions tested.

Natural flake graphite was purchased from Qingdao Jinrilai graphite Corporation. Hydrogen peroxide (30%), anhydrous ethanol and potassium permanganate were obtained from Tianjin Jiangtian Chemical Corporation. Pyromellitic acid (PMA) was purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Corporation. Rhodamine B was obtained from Tianjin Chemical Co. Ltd. Aqueous solutions of the desired ions (Na+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Hg2+) were prepared from nitrate salts. All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were recorded on a Bruker Ten-sor27 spectrometer (Germany) with a KBr disc in the wavelength range of 4000–400 cm−1. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were taken on Germany Bruker AX in the range of 5–90°. The morphology of Graphene oxide-pyromellitic acid was investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (NanoSEM 450). Fluorescence spectrums were carried out with the Hitachi F-4600.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide-pyromellitic acid-Rhodamine B dispersion
ce:section.ce:para: Graphite oxide (GO) was obtained from natural flake graphite by a modified Hummers method [1,12,19]. To be specific, 1 g of natural flake graphite, 0.5 g of sodium nitrate and 23 mL of concentrated H2SO4 were added to 250 mL round-bottom flask. After consistently stirring for 30 min in the ice-water bath, 3 g of KMnO4 was added slowly, while the system temperature was kept below 15 °C. Then, the system was heated for 30 min at 35 °C, 50 mL of distilled water was added gradually. After stirring for 15 min at 98 °C, the mixture was diluted with 140 mL of distilled water and approximate 10 mL 30% of H2O2 solution, the color of the mixture changed to a luminous yellow. Finally, the mixture centrifuged and washed with 500 mL of 5% HCl aqueous solution and distilled water. The product was dried at 50 °C for 24 h.

0.1 g GO was dispersed in anhydrous ethanol by ultrasonic stripping and 0.3387 g PMA was dissolved in anhydrous ethanol. After mixing the two dispersion liquid, the system remained ultrasonic stripping for 1 h. Then, the mixture was maintained for 12 h at room temperature, meanwhile the pH level of the mixture was adjusted to 6–7 by stronger ammonia water. The obtained mixture was washed with anhydrous ethanol and distilled water for several times. At last, the products were dried at 50 °C for 8 h.
The GOS-PMA-RB dispersion was obtained by that 0.0052 g of the obtained graphene oxide-pyromellitic acid was dispersed in 200 mL Rhodamine B ethanol solution (100 mg/L) under ultrasound.

Dispersion were divided equally, and was taken in an equal amount aqueous solution of the desired ions (Na+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Hg2+). The fluorescence spectra of all mixed solutions were recorded after each addition.

The different (10−7, 0.5*10−6, 10−6, 0.5*10−5, 10−5, 10−4, 0.5*10−3, 10−3 and 10−2 mol/L) concentration of Fe3+ solution were prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O in water and adjusting the volume to 100 mL in a volumetric flask. After different concentrations of Fe3+ aqueous solution were taken in the same amount of dispersion, the fluorescence spectra of mixed solutions were recorded.

The preparation of graphene oxide-pyromellitic acid (GOSs-PMA) was reached through a simple stirring process under room temperature. GOSs was obtained by the modified Hummers method and ultrasonic stripping [1,12,19]. PMA was dissolved in ethanol solution. They were mixed in the round-bottom flask under ambient temperature and pH was adjusted to 6–7 by stronger ammonia water. The system remains stirring for 12 h. Finally, the products were washed with anhydrous ethanol and distilled water to rule out the excess PMA and other ions. The schematic diagram of synthesis of GOSs-PMA is shown in Fig. 1 .

The FT-IR of GO and GOSs-PMA can be observed in Fig. 2 . We can find the typical characteristic spectrums of GO. The peaks at 3438 cm−1, 1732 cm−1, 1624 cm−1, 1388 cm−1 and 1045 cm−1 are attributed to OH vibration, CO stretching, CC skeletal vibration, COH stretching vibration and CO stretching vibration, respectively [16,20]. In the FT-IR spectrum of GOSs-PMA, the main characteristic peaks of PMA originating from the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COOH (1720–1680 cm−1(νCO) and 1320–1210 cm−1 (νCO)) [20,21] have shifted to 1575 cm−1 and 1381 cm−1. These shifts may be ascribed to electrostatic interactions as well as hydrogen bond between PMA and GOSs [22,23]. These shifts also indicate PMA has been deposited on the surface of GOSs successfully.

The SEM image of GO and GOSs-PMA reveals more details as shown in Fig. 3 . In Fig. 3a, the graphene oxide sheets can be observed [16]. Compared with GO (Fig. 3a), many solid particles on the surface of GOSs (Fig. 3b). These particles are randomly dispersed on the surface. As shown in Fig. 4 , the oxygen-containing functional groups of GOSs are also irregular. These phenomena indicate that the interaction between PMA and GOSs may be originated from the interactions between the oxygen-containing functional groups of GOSs and carboxyl of PMA [14,24]. This interaction is mainly the electrostatic interaction as well as hydrogen bond between them, which are proved by the changes of the FT-IR spectra in Fig. 2.

The crystal structures of PMA, GO and GOSs-PMA were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD). Their own XRD patterns were shown in Fig. 5 . In the XRD pattern of GO, there is strong and sharp diffraction peak at 11.7°, which belongs to the (001) reflection of GO [11]. After modified by PMA, the peak at 11.7° is decreased and shifted to 9.9° in the XRD pattern of GOSs-PMA. This result suggested that the existence of PMA destroyed the crystal structure of GO and has caused the interlayer space of GO increase from 0.759 nm to 0.898 nm [16]. Furthermore, we found there is a new diffraction peak at 2θ = 6.3°, which is different from the diffraction peaks of PMA and GO in the XRD pattern of GOSs-PMA. The occurrence of this new diffraction peak is a symbol of a new crystal structure formed by the interaction between PMA and GOSs. This also suggests the GOSs-PMA has been prepared successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ with graphene oxide-pyromellitic acid-Rhodamine B dispersion
ce:section.ce:para: To study the quenching effect of GOSs-PMA, the small amount of GO and GOSs-PMA was scattered in the same volume of RB ethanol solution, respectively. We wondrously found that GOSs-PMA does not quench the fluorescence of RB as shown in Fig. 6 . Their fluorescence emission spectra also proved this phenomenon in Fig. 7 . Previous studies have proven that GO can quench the fluorescence of RB due to fluorescence resonance transfer or non-radiative dipole-dipole coupling between RB and GO [16,20,25]. In our work, we received an exciting result that the fluorescence of RB is kept well. This unexpected behavior might because the deposition of PMA on the surface of GO weaken the quenching effects. PMA has taken up the surface of GO and formed a certain distance between RB and GO driven by electrostatic interaction. It is the result of these interactions that the quenching effects of GO are weakened. The change in quantum efficiency validates our conjecture. Under 365 nm excitation, the quantum efficiencies of RB, GO-RB and GOSs-PMA-RB is 52.3%, 1.5% and 31.6%, respectively. This change is mainly due to electron hole of GOSs. The photoelectron of RB can be absorbed by electron hole of GOSs. However, the presence of PMA weakens this absorption. In Fig. 8 , we show the fluorescence emission spectra of GOSs-PMA-RB and GO-RB with the different ratio of GO. We find the positive role of PMA still exists even if the ratio of GO increases.

To rationalize interactions between RB and GOSs-PMA, we can find some details from the liquid FT-IR of RB, GO-RB and GOSs-PMA-RB in Fig. 9 . Owing to the amount of solute in dispersion is very small, the absorption peaks of the solvent are shown in the picture. In particular, the peak at 1661 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO in RB. After adding GO and GOSs-PMA, the absorption intensity of CO stretching increased and the peak of CO stretching has shifted to 1659 cm−1 and 1651 cm−1, respectively. This enhancement and shift can be attributed to the electrostatic interactions as well as hydrogen bond between them [26]. And the electrostatic interactions between GOSs-PMA and RB are stronger than π-π stacking between GO and RB. This is part of the reasons for the change of quantum efficiency.

Pollutions is becoming more severe as the society develops vigorously. For the detection of metal ions becomes very important concern in living systems, organisms and environment. Recently, many researches based on RB nanoprobes have been introduced in ion detection [27–29]. Unfortunately, the quenching effect of GOSs restricts its application in ion detection of RB nanoprobes. In order to demonstrate whether GOSs-PMA-RB dispersion can maintain the ionic selectivity of RB, we investigated the metal ion sensing behavior of this dispersion. As shown in Figs. 10 and 11
, Fe3+ can cause the strongest fluorescence quenching effect after the dispersion interacting with different metal ions, including Hg2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, K+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+ and Na+. These data and phenomena indicate that this dispersion still maintains the efficient ionic selectivity of RB. This will have a certain guiding significance to expand the application of GOSs in the dye.

The luminescence quenching effect was calculated according to the values of the Stern–Volmer equation [30]:
(1)
I 0
I
= 1 +
K
S V
[ M ]
where I
0 and I are the luminescent intensities before and after metal ion incorporation, respectively; [M] is the metal ion molar concentration; K
SV is the coefficient of quenching, which can be calculated from the luminescent data. By this equation, we can evaluate the relationship between GOS-PMA-RB dispersion and Fe3+ concentration. The fluorescence intensity change of GOSs-PMA-RB after taking in Fe3+ with different concentration is revealed in Fig. S1 (in the Supporting Information). As shown in Fig. 12 , the Stern-Volmer plot can be fit with a linear correlation in the Fe3+ concentration range of 1*10−4-1 mM and the value of K
SV is as high as 45827 M−1. Such highly K
SV suggest that this dispersion has highly selective and sensitive sensing of Fe3+ in aqueous solution. Fe3+ ion is an important element in all living lives. It can help oxygen metabolism and gene synthesis. Along with the deterioration of the environment, it is easy to cause the loss of Fe3+ ion in living lives. This loss can cause many serious diseases, such as Huntington's, Parkinson's and so on [31,32]. Therefore, how to selectively detect Fe3+ ion content has become highly important to scientists. With highly selective and sensitive sensing, this dispersion shows significant advantages and value in ion detection field.

As for the quenching mechanism of GOSs-PMA-RB dispersion, we consider the structure of RB maintains even with the existence of GOSs-PMA and the main force between them is electrostatic interactions as well as hydrogen bond. The light emitting groups are not affected. However, the direct addition of different metal ions can interact with the light emitting structure of RB and cause fluorescence quenching of dispersion to different extent [31,33]. In these ions, the electron configurations of Fe3+ lead to the strongest quenching effects. The excited electrons of RB are absorbed by d orbial of Fe3+ via nonradiative electron transfer leading to fluorescence quenching [34,35]. The reaction schematic diagram is shown in Fig. 13 .

In summary, the deposition of PMA on the surface of GOSs via noncovalent approach is demonstrated. The quenching effect of this modified GOSs is significantly weakened. After the right amount of this modified GOSs dispersing in the RB ethanol solution, the fluorescence of RB was well-reserved. As a dispersion probe, the GOSs-PMA-RB solutions still have a highly sensitive and selective sensing of Fe3+ ions among 12 metal ions. This work has an important significance in the optical applications of graphene, especially in the synthesis of high luminescence intensity phosphors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Educational Committee of Hebei Province (No. ZD2017214).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2018.03.334.
